content: Hiya folks, I see ForeignOwnable was added in https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/commit/26949bac1e8264c711a81f962845aa0538279c78 with *const core::ffi::c_void in its API, but I'm wondering: could we/should we make that NonNull<c_void> instead?
It's designed to directly interoperate with C code, using NonNull would make every user have to add .as_ptr()
That's a point. We could use NonNull in one direction and not the other (only in from_foreign). Would that be better?
you need both directions though
since the pointer that comes from into_foreign gets passed to C
Sorry, what do you mean? I'm proposing that into_foreign remain unchanged but from_foreign take a NonNull instead of a raw pointer.
ah, I got it in reverse
but one calls from_foreign with a pointer coming from C
and normally one doesn't check for null

Benno Lossin said:

and normally one doesn't check for null

This is an interesting detail. Where is the code that uses this btw? Obviously not in tree, right?
yeah there are some occurrences in the old rust branch
we could use NonNull, but often one would use NonNull::new_unchecked and thus have an additional unsafe call
I'd argue that's preferable, since you want to demand extra scrutiny in those cases =/
I don't think that there is extra scrutiny, we're fully trusting the C side to provide a non-null pointer if we call new_unchecked
That's not what I mean. What I mean is that the reader would see new_unchecked and would pause to think a moment about whether the operand is really guaranteed to be non-null.

compare the two versions:

impl VTable<T: Operations> {
    extern "C" fn foo_callback(ptr: *core::ffi::c_void) {
        T::foo(unsafe { ForeignOwnable::from_foreign(NonNull::new_unchecked(ptr)) })
    }
}

impl VTable<T: Operations> {
    extern "C" fn foo_callback(ptr: *core::ffi::c_void) {
        T::foo(unsafe { ForeignOwnable::from_foreign(ptr) })
    }
}

I don't think having the new_unchecked there will add any additional scrutiny

Surely signaling in the type signature that the operand must not be null is better than not? I mean, maybe people won't squint, but they're even less likely to if the type doesn't communicate the requirement.
Some implementations of ForeignOwnable might have to use null
I don't know if we use this, but we could implement it for Option<Box<T>>
But what about try_from_foreign that uses null as a special value?
ah right we have that...
in normal code, I would agree, but I am not so sure if the hassle is worth it for this kind of glue code...
Another idea: we could change the return of into_foreign to *mut T where T is another associated type. That would elide the need for FOREIGN_ALIGN from the xarray patches.
(but would probably require some callers to write as _)
Could avoid the breakage by interposing into_foreign_foo (name to be bikeshed) which returns *mut T and make into_foreign provided using it.
You still won't get around checking the alignment in a build_assert!
Yes, that's right, but It will get around the need to make the trait unsafe (because currently it pinky-promises the alignment)
Raw pointers don't really have to be aligned, so I don't think you really get around it.
Raw pointers don't, but values do - given that into_foreign takes self you'd really have to go out of your way to return a misaligned pointer

Tamir Duberstein said:

Yes, that's right, but It will get around the need to make the trait unsafe (because currently it pinky-promises the alignment)

What trait are you talking about? ForeignOwnable is currently a safe trait.
XArray makes it unsafe
Oh, I see, but as Alice said, making into_foreign return a *mut T doesn't make it safe, because impls are free to return an unaligned pointer from into_foreign.

Boqun Feng said:

Oh, I see, but as Alice said, making into_foreign return a *mut T doesn't make it safe, because impls are free to return an unaligned pointer from into_foreign.

Yes, all true, but would require something pretty unusual (like returning an interior pointer to an unaligned field).
I don't think we want to make ArcInner public, but we would have to if we want to change it from a void pointer.

Tamir Duberstein said:

Benno Lossin said:

and normally one doesn't check for null

This is an interesting detail. Where is the code that uses this btw? Obviously not in tree, right?

XArray is a user
Oh, you were asking where there are users that do not null check?
Yep, exactly.
I misread then :upside_down:
in the old rust tree, I think fs.rs uses it
What is the old rust tree?
The branch called "rust"
the original branch: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/tree/rust
Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/branches#rust.
I wanted to have this trait actually permit null as valid value, so we can implement it for usize.
@Gary Guo can you elaborate? How would you implement it for usize?
you just turn the integer into a pointer and vice-versa (value as *const core::ffi::c_void)

Oh, and zero would be a legal value. Got it.

Such an implementation would break XArray because "You must first convert it into an entry using xa_mk_value()"[1].

[1] https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/master/Documentation/core-api/xarray.rst#:~:text=You%20must%20first%20convert%20it%20into%20an%20entry%20using%20xa_mk_value()
XArray probably need its own trait because of its tagging behaviour
It requires all pointers to be 4-byte aligned for example
Entirely separate or bounded by ForeignOwnable?
If it's bounded, we can't use NonNull. If it's not, we'll repeat a bunch of code :(
I would bound it
if we change to NonNull, we could make into_foreign return Option<NonNull<c_void>> and then we could still implement ForeignOwnable for usize.

I would of course be OK with this! The argument against that higher up was:

Benno Lossin said:

It's designed to directly interoperate with C code, using NonNull would make every user have to add .as_ptr()

another alternative would be to make it more general: make ForeignOwnable generic over P which is a pointer type. Then one can implement ForeignOwnable<*const c_void> or ForeignOwnable<NonNull<c_void>>. But I dislike that solution, since it makes it rather complicated...

Tamir Duberstein said:

I would of course be OK with this! The argument against that higher up was:

Benno Lossin said:

It's designed to directly interoperate with C code, using NonNull would make every user have to add .as_ptr()

yes and that argument still stands :)
I don't think that it's going to benefit the glue code a lot
but that might be different for XArray... I can't remember the code
What is "it" in this context? XArray just wants an invariant that pointers stored in it are 4-byte aligned
But it can also be used to store integers with some additional ceremony.

Benno Lossin said:

I don't think that it's going to benefit the glue code a lot

do you mean "it" here? I meant using NonNull isn't really going to benefit the glue code
What I was trying to say is that maybe ForeignOwnable doesn't make sense to implement for integers because e.g. XArray required special ceremony compared to use with pointers
I think that's really XArray specific, not really related to ForeignOwnable
